summary: Martin Luther (1483-1546) was the German Augustinian friar and theologian whose Ninety-Five Theses against the sale of indulgences, provoked by a dispute with the indulgence-seller Johann Tetzel, ignited the Protestant Reformation in 1517. Summoned to defend his views at the 1521 Diet of Worms, he allegedly declared "Here I stand, I can do no other" before Emperor Charles V, was placed under the imperial ban, and went into hiding at Wartburg Castle under the alias "Junker Jorg," where he translated the New Testament into German. He broke with the radical wing of the Reformation during the German Peasants' War, condemning the rebels in "Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants," and developed core doctrines like justification by faith alone (sola fide) through debates with Andreas Karlstadt, Johann Eck, and Huldrych Zwingli. A prolific hymn-writer, he composed "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God," and was closely painted by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder. Late in life he wrote the virulently antisemitic tract On the Jews and Their Lies.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Luther is identified in giveaways as the Augustinian friar and theologian whose Ninety-Five Theses ignited the Protestant Reformation. Clues cite his self-description as a "stout doctor," often depicted beside the thinner Philip Melanchthon, his marriage to the former nun Katharina von Bora (breaking clerical celibacy), his excommunication by Pope Leo X, and posthumous compilations of his remarks such as Table Talk.

clues.clue: Nailed/posted the Ninety-Five Theses to a church door in Wittenberg, sparking the Protestant Reformation
examples: name this man whose 95 Theses spurred the Protestant Reformation.
This German monk nailed the Ninety-Five Theses to the cathedral door in Wittenburg to protest the sale of indulgences.

clues.clue: Married the former nun Katharina von Bora, setting a precedent for clerical marriage
examples: This Protestant reformer set a precedent for clerical marriage by marrying Katharina von Bora.
A different artist created a double portrait of this man with his wife, who is wearing a fur coat.

clues.clue: Excommunicated/condemned by Pope Leo X after refusing to recant at the Diet of Worms
examples: This man was convicted in the Diet of Worms after Pope Leo X ("the tenth") criticized his Ninety-five Theses.
denounced by Pope Leo X at the Diet of Worms

clues.clue: Called himself a "stout doctor," often depicted in portraits beside the thinner Philip Melanchthon
examples: This self-proclaimed "stout doctor" was often depicted next to the thinner Philip Melanchthon.

clues.clue: His biographer Johannes Mathesius compiled his sayings in the book Table Talk
examples: Johannes Mathesius, the author of the Formula of Concord, wrote a popular biography of this man; Mathesius also transcribed some of this man's sayings from the book Table Talk.

works.name: Ninety-Five Theses & Indulgences Controversy
works.description: Luther's dispute with the indulgence-seller Johann Tetzel drove him to draft the Ninety-Five Theses against the sale of indulgences in 1517. The document attacked the papacy's claimed authority to remit sin for payment, drew formal condemnation from universities and eventually Pope Leo X, and was followed by A Sermon on Indulgences and Grace.

clues.clue: Written after a dispute with the indulgence-seller Johann Tetzel
examples: This man was incensed to write his most famous work by a dispute with Johann Tetzel over the sale of indulgences.

clues.clue: Attacked/criticized the Catholic Church's sale of indulgences
examples: The Universities of Leuven and Cologne were among the first institutions to condemn this religious reformer, who criticized the Catholic Church's selling of indulgences in his Ninety-Five Theses.
This German monk nailed the Ninety-Five Theses to the cathedral door in Wittenburg to protest the sale of indulgences.

clues.clue: Followed the Theses with A Sermon on Indulgences and Grace, articulating the doctrine of grace, one of the Reformation's five "solas"
examples: After penning the Ninety-Five Theses, Luther wrote A Sermon on Indulgences and this concept, one of the five "solas" of the reformation, which signifies the blessings that God gives regardless of merit.

works.name: Diet of Worms (1521)
works.description: Summoned to the imperial Diet of Worms in 1521 to answer for the Ninety-Five Theses, Luther refused to recant before Emperor Charles V, allegedly declaring "Here I stand, I can do no other." Placed under the imperial ban, he was sheltered by Elector Frederick the Wise (Frederick III of Saxony) at Wartburg Castle, where he lived under the alias "Junker Jorg."

clues.clue: Allegedly declared "Here I stand, I can do no other" when refusing to recant before the Diet
examples: what religious leader declared "Here I stand, I can do no other" at the 1521 Diet of Worms?
This man legendarily declared "Here I stand, I can do no other" at the Diet of Worms.

clues.clue: Went into hiding at Wartburg Castle under the alias "Junker Jorg," sheltered by Elector Frederick the Wise of Saxony
examples: John the Steadfast gifted this man the Black Cloisters after returning from a period of exile during which he went by "Junker Jorg."

The excommunicated Luther got to have a hearing thanks to the intervention of this elector of Saxony, who was the third of his name and known as "the Wise." This man later hid Luther in Wartburg castle.

works.name: German Peasants' War
works.description: Luther broke sharply with the radical wing of the Reformation during the 1524-25 German Peasants' War, rejecting the rebels' Twelve Articles and condemning Thomas Muntzer's uprising in the tract Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants, in which he urged the nobility to slaughter the "unruly" peasant hordes. Historians such as William Shirer have cited the episode as evidence of an authoritarian strain in Luther's thought.

clues.clue: Wrote Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants, condemning the Peasants' War and rejecting the Twelve Articles
examples: This writer rejected the Twelve Articles and the motivations of the German Peasants' War in general in his tract Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants.
This theologian attacked the peasant revolt Muntzer helped lead in "Against the Thieving, Murderous Peasants."

clues.clue: Sided with the magistrates against Anabaptist clergy and their "thieving, murderous hordes" of unruly followers
examples: This author of To the Christian Nobility of Germany sided with magistrates against Anabaptist clergy and their thousands of unruly followers in a tract against "thieving, murderous hordes of peasants."

clues.clue: William Shirer's The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich cites this tract as evidence of his "ferocious belief in absolute political authority," tracing a direct link to Hitler
examples: William L. Shirer's The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich traces a direct link between this German and Hitler, arguing that this theologian had a "ferocious [belief] in absolute political authority." That belief is supported by this man's tract, "Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants."

clues.clue: Opposed the Zwickau Prophets, Anabaptists influenced by Thomas Muntzer who believed they received messages directly from God
examples: Luther also opposed this group of Anabaptists influenced by Thomas Muntzer. This group, which included Thomas Dreschal and Nicholas Storch, believed themselves to receive messages directly from God.

works.name: Theology & Debates
works.description: Luther's central doctrine of justification by faith alone (sola fide) shaped his conflicts with fellow reformers and the Catholic hierarchy: he clashed with Andreas Karlstadt and Johann Eck at the 1519 Leipzig Debate, denied Christ's Real Presence in the Eucharist against Huldrych Zwingli at the 1529 Marburg Colloquy, and in treatises like On Temporal Authority and To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation developed the "doctrine of two kingdoms" distinguishing secular from spiritual authority.

clues.clue: In On Temporal Authority, distinguished the coercive government "of the sword" from spiritual government of the divine, the "doctrine of two kingdoms"
examples: This thinker's book On Temporal Authority proposes a distinction between the coercive government of the sword and spiritual government of the divine, commonly called the "doctrine of two kingdoms."

clues.clue: In To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation, called the exclusive papal authority to call a church council the third of "three walls" usurping political authority, and remarked that a righteous prince is a "rare bird in heaven"
examples: This thinker remarks that a prince is a "rare bird in heaven" in an address which describes the exclusive authority to call a church council as the third of "three walls" constructed to usurp political...

clues.clue: Contrasted mere tyrants with "werewolf" rulers, whom the population had the right to overthrow along with their subordinates
examples: This thinker contrasted mere tyrants with "werewolves," rulers whom the population had the right to murder along with their subordinates.

clues.clue: Wrote that a Christian is both a "lord of all" and a "servant of all," and that a believer's whole life should be one of repentance
examples: A text by this thinker opens by stating that Jesus "willed the entire life of believers to be one of repentance." This thinker wrote that a Christian is both a "lord of all" and a "servant of all."

clues.clue: At the 1519 Leipzig Debate, his ally Andreas Karlstadt argued against the necessity of penance and alms-giving opposite Johann Eck
examples: This man's pupil Andreas Karlstadt argued against the need for penance and alms giving against Johann Eck in the Leipzig Debate.
This German reformer penned 151 theses a year before Luther, who joined this man in the Leipzig Debate against Johann Eck in 1519 and sheltered this man during the Peasants' War.

clues.clue: At the 1529 Marburg Colloquy, defended sola fide and denied Christ's Real Presence in the Eucharist against Huldrych Zwingli
examples: This man also argued for the doctrine of Sola Fide and denounced the presence of the Real Christ in a debate at Marburg against Huldrych Zwingli.

clues.clue: In the Heidelberg Disputation (alongside Disputatio de Homine and Two Kinds of Righteousness), argued that humans have nothing but "empty achievements"
examples: In addition to writing Disputation Concerning Man and Two Kinds of Righteousness, this philosopher also argued that humans have nothing but "empty achievements" in his Heidelburg Disputation.

clues.clue: Wanted to remove the Book of James from the Biblical canon for contradicting his doctrine of justification by faith alone
examples: He wanted to remove the book of James because it violated his most important doctrine, justification by faith alone.

works.name: German Bible Translation (Luther Bible)
works.description: While in hiding at Wartburg Castle, Luther translated the New Testament into German using Erasmus's Greek text (the Novum Instrumentum omne) and later completed the Old Testament from Hebrew with help from his friend Johannes Bugenhagen, producing the Luther Bible. The translation prioritized vernacular clarity, controversially inserted the word "allein" ("alone") into Romans, relegated Hebrews, James, and Jude toward the back of the canon, and was illustrated with woodcuts by Lucas Cranach the Elder.

clues.clue: Translated the Bible into German, working from Hebrew for the Old Testament and drawing on Erasmus's Greek New Testament (the Novum Instrumentum omne)
examples: This book draws heavily on a Greek text known as the Novum Instrumentum omne, published by Erasmus.
This man used Erasmus's text in his own translation of the Bible into German.

clues.clue: Justified his vernacular word choices with the principle "we must ask the mother in the home, the children on the street," in a reply to Jerome Emser
examples: The language in this book was chosen on the principle that "we must ask the mother in the home, the children on the street" and was justified in a reply to Jerome Emser.

clues.clue: Controversially added the word "allein" (alone) to a key passage in Romans, with help from his town priest and friend Johannes Bugenhagen
examples: The word "allein" was controversially added to a key passage in this book.
This man's friend Johannes Bugenhagen was his town priest and helped this man with his translation of the Bible, to which he added the word "alone" in Romans.

clues.clue: Relegated the epistles of James, Jude, and Hebrews toward the end of his personal canon, calling James an "epistle of straw" for contradicting justification by faith
examples: This religious reformer devised their own canon of the Bible, relegating Jude, James, and Hebrews to the end of their German translation of the Bible, which was done in the Wartburg after they were outlawed at the Diet of Worms.
Luther was particularly hostile to the Epistle of James, describing it as 'an epistle of straw' for its contention that 'faith without [these things] is dead'.

clues.clue: Illustrated with 22 woodcuts by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder
examples: Cranach also made 22 woodcuts for the Bible translation by what German reformer?

clues.clue: Nicknamed the "September Testament" for its first printing in September 1522; introduced iconic alliterative phrases such as "Der Herr ist mein Hirte"
examples: This book introduced iconic alliterative phrases such as "Der Herr ist mein Hirte." This book is sometimes named for the month of September, when it was first printed in 1522.

clues.clue: Max Weber's The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism traces the modern capitalist notion of "vocation" (the German word Beruf) through this translation
examples: In a section of "The Protestant Ethic" titled for this man's "conception of the calling," Weber traces the modern capitalist notion of "vocation" through this church reformer's German Bible translation.

works.name: Hymns & Music
works.description: Luther was a prolific hymn-writer credited with helping invent the German chorale. His best-known hymn, "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God" (a paraphrase of Psalm 46 containing the line "a bulwark never failing"), is quoted in the finale of Felix Mendelssohn's Symphony No. 5 ("Reformation") and referenced in Julius Eastman's "Gay Guerilla." He also wrote the Christmas carol "Vom Himmel hoch, da komm ich her" and contributed to early hymnals like the Achtliederbuch ("Eight Song Book") and the Erfurt Enchiridion.

clues.clue: Composed "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God," a 1529 paraphrase of Psalm 46 containing the line "a bulwark never failing"
examples: name this reformer who wrote the hymn "A Mighty Fortress is Our God."
"A Mighty Fortress Is Our God" was written by this Protestant leader and author of the Ninety-five Theses.

clues.clue: Apocryphally justified using secular tunes for hymns by asking, "why should the Devil have all the good tunes?"
examples: When asked why he used secular songs in composing new hymns, this man apocryphally said "why should the Devil have all the good tunes?"
This composer defended his right to modify Gregorian chants by asking, "why should the devil have all the good tunes?"

clues.clue: "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God" is quoted in the finale of Felix Mendelssohn's Symphony No. 5 ("Reformation")
examples: A piece by this composer about "a bulwark never failing" is quoted in Mendelssohn's...
Quotations of a piece written by this person appear in the finale of Felix Mendelssohn's Symphony No. 5.

clues.clue: Wrote the Christmas carol "Vom Himmel hoch, da komm ich her"
examples: The carol "Vom Himmel hoch, da komm ich her" was written by this prolific hymn composer whose other hymns include "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God."

clues.clue: His works first appeared in the "Eight Song Book" (Achtliederbuch) alongside Paul Speratus, followed by a larger collection including Elisabeth Cruciger's contributions in the Erfurt Enchiridion
examples: Along with Paul Speratus, this composer's works first appeared in a collection called the "Eight Song Book" and the Erfurt Enchiridion.
Eighteen works by this composer were published alongside contributions by friends like Elisabeth Cruciger in an Enchiridion conceived as a follow-up to this man's "Eight-song Book."

clues.clue: Developed the Bar form (AAB structure) chorale often used to close cantatas; melody C-C-C-G-A-B-C-B-A-G opens one of his pieces
examples: This composer developed a genre made of three short phrases in AAB form that was often used for the last movement of cantatas.
The melody: C, C, C, G - A - B, C - B - A, long G begins a piece by this inventor of the chorale.

clues.clue: Simplified the Te Deum into a version considered his home country's rendering of the hymn
examples: This composer's simplification of the Te Deum is often called his home country's version of the hymn.

clues.clue: Praised Josquin des Prez as "the master of notes," who "bids the notes as he will" while other composers must obey the notes
examples: This person praised Josquin de Prez as "the master of notes" since "they have to do as he bids them; other composers have to do as the notes will."
This contemporary of Josquin wrote extensively in praise of his music.

clues.clue: Wrote that "next to the Word of God, music deserves the highest praise"
examples: This person wrote, "next to the Word of God, music deserves the highest praise."

clues.clue: The forest zither was wrongly named for him by Ferdinand Roese, due to a mistaken belief he was a virtuoso of it
examples: Ferdinand Roese wrongly named the forest zither after this composer due to the mistaken belief that he was a virtuoso of it.

works.name: On the Jews and Their Lies
works.description: Late in life, Luther authored the virulently antisemitic treatise On the Jews and Their Lies, which called Jewish people a "brood of vipers" (echoing Christ's words in Matthew) and urged their forced conversion or expulsion; the same period saw him dismiss the pious visions of Saint Bridget of Sweden.

clues.clue: Wrote On the Jews and Their Lies, urging the forced conversion or expulsion of Jewish people
examples: This religious leader published On the Jews and Their Lies, urging their forced conversion or expulsion.
This man wrote the treatise On the Jews and Their Lies.

clues.clue: Cited Christ's calling the Pharisees a "brood of vipers" in Matthew, writing "we are at fault for not slaying them"
examples: He notes that Christ called the title group a brood of vipers in Matthew and says "we are at fault for not slaying them" in On the Jews and Their Lies.

clues.clue: Dismissed the visions of Saint Bridget of Sweden, criticizing her "little prayers"
examples: This man also didn't think highly of Saint Bridget, and criticized teachings involving Bridget's "little prayers."

works.name: Legacy: Artistic Depictions
works.description: Luther's close friendship with the court painter Lucas Cranach the Elder produced his best-known portraits, typically showing him in a black robe and floppy hat, including panels of the Wittenberg Altarpiece and the Weimar Altarpiece. Hostile Catholic-aligned artists also caricatured him, from Hans Brosamer's seven-headed woodcut monster to Hans Holbein the Younger's image of him as a book-pointing Hercules trampling dead philosophers.

clues.clue: Painted repeatedly by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder, typically in a black robe and floppy hat
examples: This man appears in a black robe with a floppy hat in many portraits by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder.
Perhaps Cranach's most famous subject was this guy, also painted by Hans Holbein. This religious leader was almost always depicted in his floppy black hat.

clues.clue: Depicted pointing to a book as blood falls on a self-portrait of the artist in the central panel of the Weimar Altarpiece
examples: This figure points to a book as blood falls on a self-portrait of an artist in the central panel of the Weimar Altarpiece.

clues.clue: Appears in the bottom panel of the Wittenberg Altarpiece, in a crucifixion scene shared with his congregation
examples: Name this altarpiece, the bottom panel of which depicts the crucifixion occurring between a certain preacher and his audience.

clues.clue: Caricatured with bees flying around one of seven heads in a woodcut by Hans Brosamer
examples: It's not the Beast, but bees fly around one of this figure's seven heads in a woodcut by Hans Brosamer.

clues.clue: Depicted as a book-pointing Hercules trampling the dead bodies of Aristotle and four other philosophers, in a caricature by Hans Holbein the Younger
examples: The dead bodies of Aristotle and four other philosophers appear below this figure in a caricature as Hercules by Hans Holbein the Younger.

clues.clue: Often depicted with a swan, due to a prophecy made at the Council of Constance
examples: Because of a prophecy at the Council of Constance, this figure is often depicted with a swan.

clues.clue: A Hans Baldung Grien woodcut depicting him as a saint inspired a lament by Albrecht Durer
examples: A Hans Baldung Grien woodcut of this figure as a saint inspired a lament to him by Albrecht Durer.

clues.clue: A double portrait with his wife Katharina von Bora, who wears a fur coat, was created by his supporters after a hostile woodcut showed a mob storming a sheep shed
examples: Name this man, whose supporters created a woodcut showing a mob of thieves storming a sheep shed. A different artist created a double portrait of this man with his wife, who is wearing a fur coat.

works.name: Legacy: Scholarly Interpretations
works.description: Later scholars have mined Luther's psychology and influence: Erik Erikson's psychobiography Young Man Luther reads his religious crisis as an identity crisis tied to his rejection of his father's plan for him to study law and a vow made to Saint Anne during a thunderstorm, while Paul Tillich and Norman O. Brown offered further psychological readings. Max Weber's The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism traces the modern notion of a "calling" to his Bible translation.

clues.clue: Erik Erikson's psychobiography Young Man Luther interprets his religious crisis as a "second birth" identity crisis tied to his rejection of his father's plan for him to study law
examples: This man, whose identity crisis is analyzed in Erik Erikson's biography about him as a "young man," proclaimed "here I stand, I can do no other" before he was denounced by Pope Leo X.
A concept of a "second birth" is identified in this person's life in a psychological biography that analyzes how he rejected his father's plan for him to pursue a law career.

clues.clue: Vowed to Saint Anne during a thunderstorm that he would enter a monastery if he survived
examples: When caught in a thunderstorm, this man vowed to Saint Anne that, if he survived, he would commit his life to a monastery.

clues.clue: Norman O. Brown's Life Against Death compares his theology's mysticism to Freudian psychoanalysis, noting his contemplation of the devil
examples: The mysticism of this person's theology is compared to Freudian psychoanalysis in Norman Brown's Life Against Death, which notes how this person spent significant time contemplating the devil.

clues.clue: Paul Tillich's autobiographical On the Boundary contrasts the effects of socialism on his life with the effects of this man's ideas
examples: Tillich's autobiographical On the Boundary contrasts the effects that socialism had on his life with the effects of this man's ideas.

clues.clue: His complete collected works are known as the Weimarer Ausgabe
examples: The complete collection of this man's work is the Weimarer Ausgabe.

comprehensive_summary: Martin Luther (1483-1546) was a German Augustinian friar and theologian whose Ninety-Five Theses against the sale of indulgences, written after a dispute with the indulgence-seller Johann Tetzel, ignited the Protestant Reformation in 1517. Summoned to the 1521 Diet of Worms to answer for the Theses, he allegedly refused to recant before Emperor Charles V with the words "Here I stand, I can do no other," was placed under the imperial ban, and was sheltered by Elector Frederick the Wise of Saxony at Wartburg Castle under the alias "Junker Jorg." There he translated the New Testament into German from Erasmus's Greek text, later completing the Old Testament from Hebrew with help from his friend Johannes Bugenhagen to produce the Luther Bible -- a translation that controversially inserted the word "allein" into Romans, relegated James, Jude, and Hebrews toward the back of the canon, and was illustrated with woodcuts by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder. Luther's doctrine of justification by faith alone (sola fide) drove disputes across his career: alongside Andreas Karlstadt he debated Johann Eck at the 1519 Leipzig Debate, and he later denied Christ's Real Presence in the Eucharist against Huldrych Zwingli at the 1529 Marburg Colloquy. In treatises like On Temporal Authority and To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation he developed the "doctrine of two kingdoms" separating secular from spiritual government. During the 1524-25 German Peasants' War he broke sharply with the movement's radical wing, rejecting the Twelve Articles and condemning Thomas Muntzer's uprising in Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants -- an authoritarian streak later cited by historian William Shirer as a precursor to Hitler. Luther was also a prolific hymn-writer: his best-known hymn, "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God," paraphrases Psalm 46 and is quoted in the finale of Felix Mendelssohn's "Reformation" Symphony; he also wrote "Vom Himmel hoch, da komm ich her" and helped found the German chorale tradition through hymnals like the Achtliederbuch and the Erfurt Enchiridion. Late in life he wrote the virulently antisemitic treatise On the Jews and Their Lies, urging the forced conversion or expulsion of Jewish people. His close friendship with Lucas Cranach the Elder produced his iconic portraits (typically in a black robe and floppy hat) and altarpiece panels in Wittenberg and Weimar, while hostile artists caricatured him as a seven-headed monster or a book-pointing Hercules. Later scholars have probed his psychology and influence: Erik Erikson's Young Man Luther reads his crisis through a Freudian lens tied to his rejection of a legal career, and Max Weber's The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism traces the modern notion of "vocation" to his Bible translation.

Diet of Worms (1521) -- own answerline in this pull (condemned by Leo X's bull Exsurge Domine, presided over by Johann Eck); folded into Luther's page as a major biographical event, but could become a standalone European History page if clue density grows

Lucas Cranach the Elder -- own answerline across multiple bonus parts (court painter to the Electors of Saxony, Judith and Holofernes paintings, Venus and Cupid); excluded, referenced heavily in the Artistic Depictions section
Huldrych Zwingli -- rival Swiss reformer at the Marburg Colloquy, own answerline (the "Affair of the Sausages"); excluded
